name: Bright Data MCP
what_it_does: All-in-one MCP server providing AI agents with live web access capabilities including browser control, SERP search, web scraping across 100M+ proxies, and structured data extraction.
use_cases: Real-time web browsing for agents needing fresh data beyond training cutoff
Scalable web scraping with residential proxy rotation to avoid blocks
SERP monitoring and competitive intelligence from agent workflows
Structured data extraction from websites at scale
Browser automation for sites requiring JavaScript rendering
not_for: Simple static site scraping (basic HTTP clients suffice)
Legal/regulatory research requiring verified sources
Very low-volume scraping where proxy costs exceed value
best_when: An agent needs reliable, unblocked access to live web data at scale with proxy rotation.
avoid_when: You only need to scrape a handful of pages or the target site's terms prohibit scraping.

auth.notes: Bright Data API token required. Free Rapid Mode vs paid Pro Mode offer different tool sets.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Proxy and scraping errors are descriptive; rate limit errors clearly communicated
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read operations idempotent; browser sessions have state
known_agent_gotchas: Free Rapid Mode only includes 2 tools - most useful tools require paid Pro Mode
60+ tools in Pro Mode can overwhelm agent tool selection - use GROUPS/TOOLS env vars to limit scope
Scraping may violate ToS of target sites - agents should check before scraping
Residential proxy costs can escalate quickly with high-volume scraping

security_score_components.security_notes: API key auth via env var. No scope granularity. Proxy operations route through Bright Data's network - data passes through third party. Legal risks around ToS compliance are real. No compliance certifications for scraping use case.

name: Browser Tools MCP
what_it_does: Three-part system (Chrome extension + Node.js server + MCP server) that lets AI coding assistants capture screenshots, read console logs, monitor network requests, and interact with live Chrome browser tabs.
use_cases: Debugging web apps by letting AI see browser console errors and network requests
Screenshot capture of current browser state for AI visual analysis
AI-driven UI testing by reading browser DOM and console output
Real-time web page analysis without leaving the AI assistant
not_for: Headless browser automation at scale
Non-Chrome browsers
Production web scraping
best_when: AI coding assistants need to see what's happening in the developer's browser to debug or analyze web apps.
avoid_when: You need cross-browser support, headless operation, or production-scale browser automation.

auth.notes: No authentication - local three-component system. Chrome extension communicates with local Node server.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Basic error messages; connectivity errors between components can be confusing
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Screenshot and read operations are idempotent. No state modification.
known_agent_gotchas: Requires three separate components running simultaneously (Chrome extension, Node server, MCP server)
Chrome extension must be manually installed from GitHub releases
Only reads browser state - cannot navigate or click (use Playwright for interaction)
Extension must be manually refreshed when code updates
Captures only the current active Chrome tab

security_score_components.security_notes: No authentication between components - local-only but any process can access the local server. Chrome extension captures all browser tab content including sensitive pages. Read-only access reduces but doesn't eliminate risk.

name: Browser Use MCP Server
what_it_does: MCP server enabling AI agents to control web browsers via the browser-use library, with full navigation, form filling, clicking, and visual web page interaction through Playwright.
use_cases: Full browser automation for agents needing to navigate, click, and fill forms
Web research agents that need to interact with dynamic JavaScript-heavy sites
Automated form submission and data entry workflows
Testing web applications through agent-driven interaction
not_for: Stateless, API-first web data extraction (use Firecrawl)
Production scraping at scale (session-based, expensive per operation)
Sites that block headless browsers
best_when: An agent needs to interact with a web browser like a human would - clicking, typing, navigating complex flows.
avoid_when: The target site has a good API, or you need stateless high-volume scraping.

auth.notes: Requires OpenAI API key hardcoded dependency. Additional browser session auth depends on target sites.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Browser errors surfaced from Playwright; MCP-level errors are basic
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Browser interactions have persistent state - clicking submit multiple times submits multiple times
known_agent_gotchas: Hard dependency on OpenAI API key - won't work with other LLM providers despite being used from MCP clients that already have an LLM
Default VNC password 'browser-use' is a security concern if exposed on network
Browser sessions maintain state - agents must manage session lifecycle explicitly
Anti-bot measures can block headless browser sessions on many sites
High resource consumption - each session requires a full browser process

security_score_components.security_notes: Default VNC password 'browser-use' is a hardcoded security risk if exposed. OpenAI API key required and stored in environment. Browser can access any URL - significant attack surface if agent is compromised. No auth on local server.

name: Browserless
what_it_does: Hosted headless Chrome service with REST API and WebSocket connections for Puppeteer, Playwright, and Selenium, providing scalable browser automation without managing Chrome infrastructure.
use_cases: Running Puppeteer or Playwright scripts without local Chrome management
PDF generation and screenshot capture at scale
Web scraping with full JavaScript rendering
Automated form testing and visual regression testing
PDF/HTML rendering for agent document generation workflows
not_for: Simple static site fetching (curl or Firecrawl suffice)
Teams wanting local control over browser infrastructure
Very high-frequency sessions beyond plan limits
best_when: You need hosted headless Chrome for Puppeteer/Playwright without managing infrastructure, with predictable pricing.
avoid_when: You need very high concurrency or want full control over the browser environment.

auth.notes: API token as query parameter or header. Same token for all operations.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Structured error responses; timeout and concurrency errors clearly communicated
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Each browser session creates fresh state; page interactions are not idempotent
known_agent_gotchas: Concurrent session limit is the key constraint - exceeding it queues or rejects requests
WebSocket connections for Puppeteer/Playwright have connection timeouts - implement reconnection logic
API key in query parameter can appear in logs
Browser stealth mode not included by default - bot detection may block some sites
Data returned from pages is not filtered - agents must handle malicious page content

security_score_components.security_notes: SOC2 certified. API key in query parameter is a mild concern. HTTPS enforced. Browser sessions can access any URL - agents must implement URL allowlisting for security. No scope granularity.

name: BrowserStack API
what_it_does: Cloud testing platform API for running automated Selenium, Cypress, and Appium tests across 3000+ real device and browser combinations, with REST API for managing sessions, builds, and results.
use_cases: Running cross-browser Selenium test suites in CI/CD via API
Managing and retrieving test session results and screenshots
Triggering Appium mobile tests on real devices programmatically
Monitoring test build status and flaky test detection
Visual testing with screenshot comparison across browsers
not_for: Unit testing (BrowserStack is for browser/device testing only)
Very simple single-browser testing (local Chrome + Playwright suffice)
Teams with tight budgets (BrowserStack is expensive at scale)
best_when: CI/CD pipelines need comprehensive cross-browser and real device testing coverage managed programmatically.
avoid_when: You only need to test one browser or one device type, or your budget doesn't support cloud testing.

auth.notes: HTTP Basic Auth with username and access key. Same credentials for all APIs.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Structured error responses; session status and error codes well-documented
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Test session creation always creates new sessions; build management operations are not idempotent
known_agent_gotchas: Parallel sessions limited by plan - agents launching many tests simultaneously may queue or fail
Session videos and screenshots retained only 60 days - agents must download before expiry
Test results require session ID tracking - agent must store IDs from session creation
Automate and App Automate are separate product APIs with different endpoints
Local testing tunnel required for testing local/staging environments

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. SOC2, GDPR, ISO27001 certified. Basic Auth is functional. No scope granularity. Test sessions run on BrowserStack's infrastructure - code under test is transmitted to their servers.

name: Bubble API
what_it_does: Bubble's Data API and Workflow API for programmatically accessing and manipulating data in Bubble no-code applications, enabling integration with external systems and agent workflows.
use_cases: Reading and writing data from Bubble database to external agent workflows
Triggering Bubble backend workflows from external systems
Syncing Bubble app data with other SaaS tools
Building data pipelines that include Bubble-based applications
User account management in Bubble apps via API
not_for: Building new features in Bubble (that requires the visual editor)
Real-time streaming or websocket operations
Non-Bubble database operations
best_when: You have an existing Bubble app and need to integrate its data with external agent workflows or other tools.
avoid_when: You don't already use Bubble, or you need real-time updates.

auth.notes: API token in Authorization header. App-specific endpoint URL required. Data API and Workflow API use same token.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error messages can be vague; privacy rule blocks often return empty results rather than error
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Thing creation creates new records; no built-in idempotency
known_agent_gotchas: Data API must be enabled explicitly for each Data Type in Bubble app settings
Privacy rules apply to API calls - 'no results' may mean privacy restriction, not empty data
App-specific URL means agents need to know the Bubble app's domain
Field names in API use slug format (underscore_case) not display names
Workflow API endpoints are named by the developer - not standardized across apps

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. SOC2, GDPR compliant. Single API key with full data access - no fine-grained scope control. Privacy rules in Bubble provide some data access control.

name: Build with Claude
what_it_does: Curated discovery hub and plugin marketplace for the Claude ecosystem, aggregating plugins, tools, prompts, and resources for Claude users and developers.
use_cases: Discovering Claude plugins and extensions for specific workflows
Browsing community-contributed Claude resources
Finding Claude-compatible tools and integrations
not_for: Direct API integration
Production tool deployment without further vetting
Authoritative Claude documentation (use Anthropic docs)
best_when: You want to discover community-built Claude tools and resources.
avoid_when: You need authoritative Anthropic documentation, security-vetted tools, or direct API access.

known_agent_gotchas: Not an MCP server - it's a discovery website
Plugin installation relies on /plugin marketplace command which may not be available in all Claude Code versions
Third-party plugins listed are not vetted by Anthropic

security_score_components.security_notes: Static website. No API surface. Third-party tools listed may have varying security postures - not vetted by Anthropic.

name: Bunny CDN
what_it_does: Developer-friendly CDN and edge storage platform with APIs for managing CDN zones, storage zones, video streaming, and DNS at competitive pricing with global edge network.
use_cases: Serving static assets via CDN for agent-generated content
Edge storage for files with global CDN delivery
Video transcoding and streaming via Bunny Stream API
DNS management for custom domain routing
Cache management and purging from agent deployment workflows
not_for: Enterprise CDN with dedicated support (Akamai or Fastly are better)
Complex WAF or DDoS mitigation requirements
Teams needing FedRAMP or HIPAA compliance
best_when: Cost-conscious teams need a performant CDN with simple API access and competitive pricing.
avoid_when: You need enterprise compliance, dedicated WAF, or Akamai-level global footprint.

auth.notes: AccessKey header for all API operations. Storage zone passwords separate from account key.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Standard HTTP error codes with JSON body; generally clear
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: File uploads and CDN purges are idempotent. Zone creation requires unique names.
known_agent_gotchas: No official SDK - agents must build their own HTTP client integration
Storage zones and CDN pull zones are separate concepts - agents must understand the model
Different API endpoints for account-level vs storage-level operations
Cache purge propagation is not instantaneous - may take seconds to minutes globally
AccessKey and storage passwords are different credentials

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. GDPR compliant. API key via header. Limited scope separation between account key and storage passwords. No FedRAMP or HIPAA - not suitable for regulated workloads.

name: Cal.com API
what_it_does: Cal.com's open-source scheduling API for managing availability, event types, bookings, and calendar integrations, available as self-hosted or cloud-hosted with OAuth2 and API key auth.
use_cases: Creating and managing bookings programmatically for agent scheduling workflows
Checking availability and free/busy slots for meeting scheduling agents
Managing event types and routing forms for automated scheduling
Syncing bookings with external calendars via integrations
Building custom scheduling UIs on top of Cal.com infrastructure
not_for: Non-scheduling workflows
Teams needing Calendly-compatible API (different API structure)
Very simple scheduling without Cal.com account
best_when: You need a flexible, open-source scheduling API with self-hosting option and good developer docs.
avoid_when: You need Calendly-compatible integrations or don't want to manage Cal.com infrastructure.

auth.notes: API key for personal/developer use. OAuth2 for multi-user apps. v1 and v2 APIs have different auth patterns.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Standard REST errors; some v1/v2 discrepancies in error formats
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Booking creation is not idempotent - retries create duplicate bookings
known_agent_gotchas: Two API versions (v1, v2) with different auth and capabilities - documentation can be confusing
Self-hosted deployments require maintaining your own infrastructure and SSL
Booking creation doesn't have built-in idempotency - duplicate bookings possible on retry
OAuth app requires Cal.com team approval for production scope access
Availability endpoint requires understanding of user's schedule configuration

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. SOC2, GDPR compliant. OAuth2 with scopes for multi-user scenarios. API key for personal use has full account access.

name: Calendly API
what_it_does: Calendly's REST API for managing scheduling links, event types, scheduled events, and invitees, enabling agents to automate scheduling workflows and retrieve booking data.
use_cases: Retrieving scheduled meetings and invitee details for CRM sync agents
Monitoring booking activity and availability via webhooks
Generating one-off scheduling links for specific agent-triggered workflows
Querying scheduled event data for reporting and analytics
Canceling or rescheduling meetings from agent workflows
not_for: Creating event types via API (read-only for event type management)
White-label scheduling UX without Calendly branding
Complex custom scheduling logic beyond Calendly's features
best_when: You use Calendly and need to integrate scheduling data into agent workflows or sync with other systems.
avoid_when: You need to fully control the scheduling UX, need Calendly-free scheduling, or need to create event types via API.

auth.notes: OAuth2 for multi-user apps. Personal access token for single-user/developer use. User-specific resources require authenticated user context.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Standard REST errors with descriptive messages; webhook validation errors can be unclear
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: API is primarily read-only for scheduling data; one-off links are single-use
known_agent_gotchas: Rate limit of 250 req/hour is very restrictive for agents processing many bookings
Cannot create event types via API - only manage existing ones
OAuth requires user to authorize each Calendly account - no service account model
Event cancellation sends notifications to invitees - agents must handle carefully
Webhook signature validation required for security - easy to skip and create vulnerability

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA BAA available. OAuth2 with scopes. Webhook signature validation documented. Rate limits enforced.
